summary: High‑energy second set with soaring Watchtower and tight Let It Grow
blurb: The show bursts with fire from the opening “Picasso Moon” through a monster “Sugaree”. The second set shines on “Foolish Heart”, “Eyes Of The World”, and an unforgettable Space → All Along The Watchtower transition, while Jerry’s slide solo and Bruce’s front‑stage piano add extra sparkle.
review: August 13, 1991 at Cal Expo delivered a summer classic that still feels fresh. The first set kicks off with a bright “Picasso Moon” and rolls through “Sugaree”, “Candyman”, “High Time” and the soaring “Let It Grow”. The real fireworks erupt in Set 2: “Foolish Heart” launches into a seamless chain of “Saint Of Circumstance”, “Eyes Of The World”, the Drums/Space duo, and a mind‑blowing Space → All Along The Watchtower transition that many fans call a highlight of the year. Jerry Garcia is on fire – his slide solo on “Sugaree” and soaring leads on “Watchtower” and “Black Peter” draw consistent praise, even if one muddy tape notes occasional tonal dips. Bruce Hornsby’s Baldwin piano sits front‑stage, adding joyful energy (though a few soundboard mixes flag it as monitor‑like). Phil Lesh’s bass shines clear in the mix, grounding the jam-heavy moments, while both Mickey Hart and Bill Kreutzmann drive a powerful Drums/Space segment that lifts the second half. Overall the performance is tight, loose‑in‑the‑right‑places, and packed with the kind of improvisational peaks that make this era memorable.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Across seven recordings the sentiment is overwhelmingly positive or mixed with no consistent criticism. Multiple sources laud the “Foolish Heart”, “Let It Grow”, and the Space → All Along The Watchtower transition, while Jerry’s playing receives repeated praise. Only occasional mix‑balance complaints appear, which affect recording choice but not show quality. This steady chorus of acclaim justifies a solid positive adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Clear soundboard capture with strong instrument separation; despite a few drop‑outs it presents the full set and showcases Jerry’s lead work and Hornsby’s piano.

key_highlights: Second‑set fire: Foolish Heart → Saint Of Circumstance → Eyes Of The World → Drums/Space → All Along The Watchtower
"Let It Grow" and "Sugaree" deliver standout vocal and guitar moments
Jerry’s slide solo on Sugaree and soaring leads on Watchtower and Black Peter
Bruce Hornsby’s front‑stage piano adds joyful energy throughout

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia is on fire, delivering a slide solo on Sugaree and soaring leads on Watchtower and Black Peter; his tone stays strong despite occasional mud in lower‑quality tapes.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh’s bass cuts through clearly, adding depth and grounding the jam-heavy sections; several reviewers note he plays particularly well throughout.
band_performance.Bob: Weir provides solid rhythm guitar work, holding down the first set and keeping energy high, though one review mentions a slight rasp toward the end.
band_performance.Mickey: Hart (alongside Bill) drives an inspiring Drums/Space segment that lifts the second half; pre‑drums Foolish Heart also gets highlighted.
band_performance.Billy: Kreutzmann’s drumming pairs with Mickey to create a powerful, dynamic percussion foundation, especially evident in the post‑drums Black Peter/Sugar Magnolia flow.
